_provenance.note: This Stage 1 JSON was reconstructed from the currently-published cards so external Stage 2 (Gemini bias) and Stage 3 (Codex fact-check) reviews can verify what readers see. It is not the original analysis — the original pipeline was not run on this issue.

headline: Civil servant WFH: fuel rationing, workplace reform, or both?
context: Phased work-from-home for non-critical civil servants was announced on 26 March 2026, the same day as the RON95 quota cut. Frontline services are excluded. No implementation details, timelines or productivity metrics have been published.

cards.text: Civil servant WFH was announced on the same day as the fuel quota cut, bundled with Raya open-house cancellations and minister travel bans.
cards.sub: The trigger is fuel conservation amid the Hormuz crisis. A crisis-born policy can still yield real reform, if it comes with targets and honesty.

cards.h: Frontline workers excluded: nurses, teachers, police, disproportionately female, rural, and the longest commuters.
cards.s: Senior Grade 41+ administrative officers (more urban, male-dominated at the top) gain flexibility. The fuel-saving logic is undermined by its own targeting.
cards.d: Frontline workers excluded: nurses, teachers, police, disproportionately female, rural, and the longest commuters. Senior Grade 41+ administrative officers (more urban, male-dominated at the top) gain flexibility. The fuel-saving logic is undermined by its own targeting.

cards.h: 1.7 million civil servants for 33 million people: one of the highest ratios globally. COVID-era WFH failed when supervisors couldn't manage remotely.
cards.s: Wage bill exceeds RM41B plus RM23B in pensions. Digital infrastructure outside Putrajaya is patchy. Without metrics, WFH risks the COVID pattern.
cards.d: 1.7 million civil servants for 33 million people: one of the highest ratios globally. COVID-era WFH failed when supervisors couldn't manage remotely. Wage bill exceeds RM41B plus RM23B in pensions. Digital infrastructure outside Putrajaya is patchy. Without metrics, WFH risks the COVID pattern.

cards.h: The UK runs a mature hybrid model with attendance targets. Singapore mandates flexible-work request rights. Malaysia has published nothing.
cards.s: Melaka, Penang and Sarawak want to act but have no federal framework. A Putrajaya guideline won't fit Kapit.
cards.d: The UK runs a mature hybrid model with attendance targets. Singapore mandates flexible-work request rights. Malaysia has published nothing. Melaka, Penang and Sarawak want to act but have no federal framework. A Putrajaya guideline won't fit Kapit.

cards.text: The real question is whether a civil service that couldn't sustain WFH during COVID can sustain it now, and whether anyone will measure.
Reform needs targets, infrastructure and honest communication. What Malaysia announced is none of these. It is a crisis response wearing reform's clothes.

confidence: Low — derived from published cards, original brief not on disk; submit for retroactive Stage 2/3 verification
